nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving rainforest streams and tributaries with sandy or fine-gravel substrates, leaf litter, submerged roots, and overhanging vegetation; typically soft, slightly acidic, tannin-stained water

decorPreferences: caves and ceramic tubes for spawning (essential)
fine sand or smooth gravel substrate
driftwood and bogwood for territory boundaries
dense planting with broad-leaved plants (Amazon sword, Java fern)
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves) to lower pH and provide cover
multiple broken line-of-sight zones to reduce pair aggression toward tankmates

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of foods: high-quality sinking cichlid pellets or micro-pellets as a staple; frozen and live foods (bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, white worms) strongly recommended to condition breeding pairs. Also accepts blanched vegetables and spirulina wafers. Forages naturally along the substrate; avoid floating foods. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily.

goodWith: small to medium West African tetras (Congo tetra, Phenacogrammus)
small peaceful catfish (dwarf corydoras, Synodontis petricola)
upper-water schooling fish that occupy different tank zones
other non-cichlid mid- to top-water species in larger tanks
avoidWith: other bottom-dwelling cichlids competing for territories

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo gallery for Pelvicachromis sacrimontis (species ID 5696). Check for CC-licensed images by researcher contributors.
Search Wikimedia Commons for available CC-licensed images of P. sacrimontis. Species is uncommon in trade so image availability may be limited.

summary: Larger Nigerian relative of the common Krib, reaching ~13 cm with iridescent green flanks and a vivid belly patch in females. Described in 1976, it vanished from the hobby for decades before a trade revival in the 2010s. A devoted cave-spawning monogamous pair-bonder — must never be mixed with P. pulcher or P. taeniatus due to hybridization risk.
careNotes:
Pelvicachromis sacrimontis, the Green Kribensis, is a Nigerian cichlid described by Roberts (1976) and notably larger than the familiar common Kribensis (P. pulcher) — males can reach 13 cm versus P. pulcher’s ~10 cm. After near-complete disappearance from the hobby for several decades, the species saw renewed availability through specialist importers in the 2010s, with multiple wild locality forms collectively traded as “Nigeria Green.”

A 120 L tank (minimum 100 cm length) suits a single bonded pair, but larger tanks of 200 L+ are advisable if tankmates are kept. Provide multiple caves — half-coconut shells, ceramic tubes, or slate arches — as the pair will claim one as a spawning site and may vigorously defend the surrounding territory during breeding. Fine sand is preferred; the fish sifts the substrate during foraging. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–7.0, gH 2–10) mirrors wild conditions and encourages breeding, though the species tolerates neutral and moderately hard water in non-breeding situations. Tannin additions via Indian almond leaves or alder cones are beneficial.

The critical husbandry warning: P. sacrimontis hybridizes readily with P. pulcher (common Kribensis) and P. taeniatus — these species must never be housed together under any circumstances. Given the species’ rarity and conservation value, hybrid contamination of captive lines is a serious concern among specialist keepers.

Buy as a proven bonded pair where possible. Established pairs are highly cohesive; splitting a bonded pair is strongly discouraged. Pairs are bi-parentally territorial during spawning and will chase tankmates away from their cave; choose robust upper-water dither fish (e.g., Congo tetras) that occupy a different zone and help the pair feel secure enough to spawn. Water quality should be kept consistently high with regular partial water changes.
breedingNotes: Pelvicachromis sacrimontis is a biparental cave-spawner that forms a strong monogamous pair bond. Conditioning with live or frozen foods (bloodworms, brine shrimp) and a slight temperature raise to 26–28°C, combined with very soft, acidic water, typically triggers spawning. The female deposits 50–150 adhesive eggs on the ceiling or walls of a chosen cave; both parents guard the eggs and fan them with their fins. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours; the wriggling fry are often moved to pits dug in the substrate by the parents before becoming free-swimming at around 5–7 days. Both parents actively defend the fry and may herd them through the tank for several weeks. Fry accept micro-worms, Artemia nauplii, and finely crushed flake from first feeding. Separate the pair from community tankmates during and after spawning or provide a heavily decorated species-specific tank to avoid escalation of territory defense. Brood sizes are moderate and manageable for the home aquarist. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate owing to the pair-bonding requirement, water chemistry sensitivity, and the need for appropriate cave furnishings.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pelvicachromis sacrimontis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males to 13 cm, females smaller), temperature 24–28°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 1–15 dH, native range (Nigeria), habitat, breeding behaviour (cave-spawner, biparental), and species incompatibility notes (hybridizes with P. pulcher and P. taeniatus).

additional.name: FishBase — Pelvicachromis sacrimontis
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Perciformes), adult size, native range (Nigeria), original description (Roberts, 1976), and water parameter ranges. Confirmed no synonym conflicts.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Kribensis Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: General Pelvicachromis care guide covering cave-spawning requirements, pairing behaviour, tank setup recommendations, and diet. Used to cross-check tank size recommendations and feeding guidance applicable to P. sacrimontis given its close relationship to P. pulcher.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Kribensis species profiles
additional.notes: Covers multiple Pelvicachromis species including sacrimontis. Confirmed hybridization risk between P. sacrimontis, P. pulcher, and P. taeniatus; trade revival in 2010s via specialist importers; multiple Nigerian locality forms; larger size than common Krib noted.

additional.name: Cichlid Room Companion — Pelvicachromis sacrimontis
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid reference confirming original description (Roberts 1976), locality data for Nigerian populations, captive breeding notes, and species-level incompatibility with congeners. Supplemented hybridization avoidance guidance.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawner; biparental pair bond. Condition with live/frozen foods; soft acidic water triggers spawning. 50–150 eggs; both parents guard and fan eggs; fry free-swimming ~5–7 days post-hatch. Separate from community tankmates during breeding.
